summary: I work for the Service Employees International Union 1199 Northwest, a healthcare local, where I serve as the Racial Justice and Education Director. I've been with the union for about 11 years now, with an earlier stint of three to four years. My role involves organizing workshops, especially around racial justice, and supporting healthcare workers across Washington and even Montana. The pandemic really shifted how we operate, moving everything online and adapting our training methods to Zoom and digital formats.

Our union office is located in Renton, Washington, and before the pandemic, I would often drive there or to local hospitals for meetings. Renton served as a central hub where members from across the state, and even from Montana, would sometimes fly or drive in for in-person workshops. Since the pandemic, though, most of our activities shifted online, reducing the need for travel to Renton.

Our union has expanded to include members in Montana, which has been a really exciting development. Because of this, our workshops now have to accommodate people joining from different states, which is easier with our shift to online formats like Zoom. This expansion shows how our union is growing beyond just Washington and adapting to new ways of connecting members.

At the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, I was working with members at Swedish Medical Center during contract bargaining. It was a challenging time because we had to quickly negotiate protections for healthcare workers amidst unclear safety protocols. One key fight was ensuring environmental services workers there received proper PPE like N95 masks, which set a precedent for other workers to demand similar protections.

The COVID-19 pandemic drastically changed how I and my union operate. We moved from in-person meetings and workshops to fully online formats, using Zoom and digital tools to continue our work. The pandemic also exposed major challenges for healthcare workers, especially around safety and PPE, and it intensified the need for unions to fight for fair treatment, hazard pay, and vaccine access. It also brought new challenges like addressing anti-Asian xenophobia and supporting members through unprecedented times.

During the pandemic, the rise of the Black Lives Matter movement was a significant moment that intersected with our union's racial justice work. We made sure to apply a racial justice lens to everything we do and created spaces for members to discuss not only the pandemic but also systemic racism. Standing up for Black lives became part of our broader commitment to racial justice within the labor movement.

At the beginning of the pandemic, Donald Trump referred to COVID-19 as the 'Wuhan flu,' which fueled anti-Asian xenophobia. This rhetoric had real impacts on Asian Americans, including myself, as I experienced anti-Asian hate firsthand in Seattle. His language contributed to a rise in discrimination and hate incidents that our union had to address alongside public health concerns.

Seattle is where I live and work, and it was also where I experienced anti-Asian hate early in the pandemic. I remember walking down Broadway in Seattle in January 2020 when I encountered hostility linked to the rising xenophobia at that time. Seattle has been a focal point for many of the issues our union addresses, from healthcare worker protections to racial justice and pandemic response.

Broadway in Seattle is where I had a personal experience with anti-Asian hate at the end of January 2020. I was simply walking down the street after getting coffee when two men passed by and made me feel targeted. This incident highlighted the rise of xenophobia in our community during the early days of the pandemic.

Bay Area Rapid Transit wasn't directly mentioned in my work or experiences, so I don't have a personal connection or story about BART. My focus has been primarily on healthcare workers and union activities in Washington and Montana, especially during the pandemic.

The 2021 Atlanta spa shootings were part of the broader context of anti-Asian violence that surged during the pandemic. While I didn't experience this event directly, it underscored the urgency of our union's work to address racism and xenophobia. These tragic events have motivated us to create spaces for dialogue and support for Asian American members.

Yellow fever wasn't something I discussed or encountered in my work or experiences. My focus has been on contemporary issues like COVID-19 and racial justice rather than historical diseases like yellow fever.
